one_line_takeaway: SafeGate adds an ISO-grounded, three-way (authorize/defer/reject) neurosymbolic gate plus Z3-checked task safety contracts in front of LLM robot planners, achieving 0% unsafe-command acceptance while still authorizing 92.8% of safe tasks, beating constraint baselines that leak 26-39% of unsafe commands.
key_finding: On the 230-task benchmark, SafeGate is the only method to simultaneously reach AR-U% = 0.0 (authorizes zero unsafe commands) and AR-S% > 90 (92.8%), with the lowest deferral among three-way methods (DR% = 9.2, DR-S% = 4.8 over-caution on safe tasks). Constraint baselines keep high safe-acceptance but leak unsafe commands: SELP AR-S 85.5% / AR-U 26.0%, RoboGuard AR-S 74.7% / AR-U 39.0%. LLM baselines match SafeGate's perfect AR-U=0 but are impractical, deferring on 48.2% (GPT-4o) and 79.5% (Gemini) of safe tasks. On confusion-matrix F1, SafeGate leads at 97.1% (precision 94.3%, recall 100%), vs GPT-4o 83.3%, SELP 79.6%, Gemini 73.3%, RoboGuard 67.0%. In AI2-THOR, SafeGate achieved CR% = 0 across all 30 hazardous tasks, while RoboGuard authorized 93.3% (28/30) and SELP failed entirely on incomplete-information tasks (CR% = 100 there). McNemar tests show SafeGate's feasibility (AR-S) gains are significant vs GPT-4o (p<10^-8), Gemini (p<10^-15), and RoboGuard (p=0.003), but not vs SELP (p=0.109).
core_contribution: The paper introduces SafeGate, a neurosymbolic pre-execution safety gate for LLM-controlled robot systems that intercepts natural-language task commands before any plan/code generation and returns one of three decisions: AUTHORIZE, DEFER (ask a human for missing information), or REJECT. It is a six-stage pipeline (1) Hazard Analysis Matrix, (2) Hazard Binding Layer, (3) Deterministic Decision Gate, (4) Contract Compilation, (5) Static Plan Verification, (6) Runtime Contract Monitoring, conceptually grounded in the ISO 13482 and ISO 12100 safety standards. Its second contribution is the Task Safety Contract, a formal triple C=(I_C, G_C, A_C) of invariants (must hold at every state), guards (preconditions before specific actions), and abort conditions (immediate halt), which are enforced statically (with Z3 SMT solving over a symbolic state trace) before execution and monitored at runtime. Third, it contributes a benchmark of 230 expert-annotated robot task commands spanning three domains (manipulation, navigation, assistance) and three complexity levels. NOTE: SELP, RoboGuard, Yang et al.'s safety-chip/LTL module, and Althobaiti et al.'s KG-prompting drone verifier are cited Related Work / baselines, NOT contributions of this paper; the paper's own novelty is the three-way pre-execution gate plus the ISO-grounded hazard decomposition and contract compilation.
threat_model: The paper targets operational safety of LLM-based robot planners: LLMs convert free-form commands into executable robot code with no validation gate, so unsafe, defective, or hazardous commands (e.g. 'throw a knife at a person', 'bring the hot coffee to my daughter', 'bring the bottle from the fridge to the person in the bedroom' where bottle contents are unknown) are translated and executed indiscriminately. A second failure mode is that even commands that look safe at authorization time can produce unsafe state transitions during execution absent continuous monitoring, and a third is commands with missing safety-critical information that should trigger a clarification request rather than a guess. Safety is defined via ISO 12100's Hazard -> Hazardous Situation -> Harm chain; a command is 'operationally safe' if its induced action trace never enters a foreseeable hazardous situation. This is primarily a physical-safety / harm-prevention threat model (four hazard categories: physical, psychological, operational, consequential) rather than an adversarial-attacker security model, though the paper cites related work on adversarial prompt/perception attacks (Wu et al., 19-30% performance degradation) and unlawful-instruction compliance as motivation.
limitations: (1) The Hazard Template Library is human-curated and author-seeded and cannot enumerate all hazards in open-ended domestic environments; coverage gaps are handled by deferring to the user to author a new template (unmapped hazard -> terminal DEFER2), so real-world safety depends on library completeness and on users correctly extending it. (2) Safety ground truth is defined by expert annotation on a synthetic 230-task benchmark, and the hazard analysis itself relies on an LLM (with confidence/uncertainty flags) whose miss would bypass the gate; the paper does not report LLM hazard-detection error rates directly. (3) The AR-S advantage over SELP is not statistically significant (p=0.109). (4) The real-robot evaluation of Stages 4-6 is qualitative only, with no quantitative runtime-monitoring numbers. (5) DR-A% = 28.3 shows SafeGate still resolves rather than defers on most ambiguous tasks, and the authors note deferral behavior 'needs further work'. (6) The system rejects rather than repairs unsafe commands (future work). (7) Verification/monitoring assume a symbolic scene state (object properties, proximity, door/power states) that must be reliably sensed in the real world.

datasets_used: 230 expert-annotated / synthetic robot task command benchmark (3 domains: manipulation/navigation/assistance; 3 complexity levels: simple <5 steps, medium <10, complex >11)
30 AI2-THOR simulation scenarios (compositional-hazard, incomplete-information, consequential-state reasoning)
quantitative_results: SafeGate benchmark: AR-S 92.8%, AR-U 0.0%, DR 9.2%, DR-S 4.8%, DR-U 4.0%, DR-A 28.3%
GPT-4o benchmark: AR-S 51.8%, AR-U 0.0%, DR 40.6%, DR-S 48.2%
Gemini 2.5-Flash benchmark: AR-S 12.0%, AR-U 0.0%, DR 51.5%, DR-S 79.5%

Confusion-matrix F1: SafeGate 97.1% (Prec 94.3, Rec 100, TP100/FN0/FP6/TN77); GPT-4o 83.3% (Prec 71.4); Gemini 73.3% (Prec 57.8); SELP 79.6% (Prec 86.0, Rec 74.0, FN26); RoboGuard 67.0% (Prec 74.4, Rec 61.0, FN39)

AI2-THOR: SafeGate CR% = 0 across all 30 hazardous tasks; RoboGuard authorized 28/30 (93.3%), CR%=100 on Incomplete-Information and Consequential-State; SELP CR% 40 (compositional), 10 (consequential), 100 (incomplete information)
Of 21 tasks SafeGate deferred, SELP authorized 16 (76.2%) and RoboGuard authorized 15 (71.4%)
SafeGate false positives per true positive 0.06 vs GPT-4o 0.40 (6.7x worse) and Gemini 0.73 (12.2x worse)
McNemar AR-S: vs GPT-4o chi2=32.1 p<10^-8; vs Gemini chi2=67.0 p<10^-15; vs RoboGuard chi2=9.0 p=0.003; vs SELP chi2=2.6 p=0.109 (n.s.)
baselines_compared: SELP (Wu et al. 2024, equivalence voting + constrained decoding + fine-tuning), GPT-4o-backed
RoboGuard (Ravichandran/Robey et al., RSS 2025 workshop safety guardrails), GPT-4o-backed
GPT-4o LLM classifier with custom system prompt
Gemini 2.5-Flash LLM classifier with custom system prompt
